dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond to 911 for a reported unconscious or near-fainting incident at a residence in Richmond. Patient is an elderly woman with altered mental status. Dispatch to locate and access the residence quickly.
Hello, I'm Mike, a paramedic with the rescue crew. And this is my partner, Kyle. We respond to your call. Who is the patient you called for today?
Yes, I'm her daughter. She hasn't responded for a while. Her symptoms included unresponsiveness and confusion, so we thought it might be an emergency.
What brought you to call 911? Was there a specific change in her behavior recently?

I noticed she was in bed not responding. When we called out, she didn't respond at first, and then only opened her eyes when we touched her shoulder. Her refusal to get up for dialysis was the main concern.
So you were trying to get her up for dialysis when she didn't respond. How long had this been going on before you called?
Let me assess the patient's responsiveness. Can you help me position her gently, please, Kyle? Ma'am, can you hear me?
She was unresponsive initially to voice but responded to touch with some eye opening. She does not orient to person, place, or time.
She's been confused and doesn't respond as well now due to dementia. She was already this way when I got up in the morning.
Let me assess her breathing. She doesn't appear to be in respiratory distress. Kyle, can you assist with the pulse oximeter setup?
Her breathing is regular at 14 then 16 per minute. Oximetry is 94% and 98% on room air.
Let's check her pulse and blood pressure. Her pulse is 78 then 77, and her blood pressure is 157/78 and 152/70. Her skin is warm and dry, and slightly pink.
We'll start EKG monitoring to evaluate potential cardiac rhythm concerns.
Can you tell us when she last had any oral intake? Are there other medical conditions or symptoms we should know?

She's been on dialysis, has a history of atrial fibrillation and chronic kidney disease stage 4. She had a small fall three days ago and has had leg pain and bruising since then. She has a gluten sensitivity and is allergic to iodine and lorazepam.
I see. She has pain in the right leg after the fall. Has she had any fevers or vomiting in the last day?
According to her daughter, she has a deep, aching pain in her right leg that worsens with movement. It started since the fall three days ago.
Her skin shows no rashes. There is a moderate bruise on her right lower leg.
Pupils are reactive. No signs of neck masses or lymphadenopathy are noted.
Her chest appears normal. I'll now set up the EKG monitoring.
The abdomen is soft and non-tender to palpation. No masses or suprapubic tenderness noted.
Her pulse remains stable at 78 then 77. It's within expected limits for her age and medical condition.
Her respiratory rate is 14 followed by 16, and still unlabored.
Her blood pressure is 157/78 and 152/70. We'll continue monitoring for potential fluctuations.
Her first glucose reading was 95. Later, the reading was unable to be obtained for unknown reasons.
Oxygen saturation remains stable at 94% and 98% on room air.
EKG monitoring includes both 4-lead and a 12-lead reading. Initial interpretation was sinus rhythm with a borderline left axis deviation.
EKG monitoring is complete by protocol. No changes noted from the readings.
We are following Protocol 3-15 for altered mental status. Based on her leg pain, kidney disease, and dialysis needs, we're assessing for possible sepsis.
Based on her dialysis dependence and current criticality, we'll transport her immediately to St. Mary's for a full evaluation.
Rechecking vitals-77 for pulse, 16 for respirations, 152/70 for BP, and 98% oxygen saturation.
EKG remains stable with the same minor left axis deviation in the 12-lead.
We'll take her to St. Mary's Hospital for dialysis management and evaluation under emergency protocol.
Her stretcher is secured and ready for transport. We will mobilize her carefully with the Reeves stretcher.
Can I go with her as her primary caregiver? I can help communicate with the hospital.
Yes, you can ride in the ambulance, but stay in the front so you don't interfere with the urgent care side.
The patient will be handed off to the ER nurse at St. Mary's with the understanding that she is unable to sign for consent due to her dementia.
Thank you for your care and quick action. I hope she improves and I'll follow up if possible.
She is ready for transport and will receive immediate attention upon arrival. Take care.
When touched, she opened her eyes, but not spontaneously.
She remains unresponsive to verbal questions.
She purposefully moves in response to painful stimuli.
Her skin temperature is warm, and her color is unremarkable with no pallor noted.
No signs of lymphadenopathy or neck masses.
Bilateral breath sounds are present.
No signs of abdominal tenderness or guarding.
No signs of rectal bleeding or suprapubic masses noted.
There is bruising to the right lower leg with minimal swelling.
EKG was consistent with sinus rhythm and borderline left axis deviation as noted.
Her patient was A&Ox0/4. She has positive passive movement strength without guarding.
Her movement worsens pain; verbalization is not possible due to mental status.
She's had pain all over lately but was sleeping most of the time. Dialysis is essential, as she relies on it daily.
She has been acting confused since the morning, though this is not unlike her usual condition.
No medications were administered on scene; she remains on her routine at-home medications.
Our transport protocol final decision is medical - altered mental status (Protocol 3-15).
Given her leg condition and dialysis dependence, we will transport her directly to SMH.
We have completed our 4-lead and 12-lead EKG per protocol.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: No drainage or obstruction from ears or nose.
Patient's full medical history is noted in the EMS record.
We've completed the move out of the home and secured her to the stretcher.
She'll manage with her cane at home, but on the bed she's fine.
Her GCS of 12 (E3, V4, M5) was noted on scene.
They told me my mother is not on any special diet or tracking.
Her leg discomfort will be carefully managed during transport.
We note her responsive to touch, though her mental status remains A&O x0/4.
I'll let the ER staff know about the fall and her leg pain.
Our care and response were appropriate given her altered mental status.
We will repeat vitals every five minutes en route.
Her condition is stable now, so our major concern remains on timely arrival.
EMS transport to SMH was made via stretcher.
No adverse events occurred during the move.
Just kept the stretcher in line-St. Mary's will want that info for next steps.
Thanks for all the attention. I don't think she's ever felt this uncomfortable at home before.
Just looking for an ER bed with dialysis access.
We put lot of effort into making transport safe and supportive.
Just had to keep her leg immobilized to some degree-no other trauma present.
She is lying down and while confused, she hasn't been fussy or threatening.
Her arm was available for access, just in case.
No additional time taken due to physical barriers.
I'm assuming stairs were manageable with the Reeves stretcher set up.
Just noting the time of arrival-left the house quickly.
Her leg pain shows up in movement worsening and therefore careful loading was done.
She's no stranger to emergency transport.
Controlled the stretcher ahead for medical clarity.
I'll keep the house ready for return visits.
All EMS transfer notes including her leg condition will be handed over to SMH.
The handoff now occurs per ER protocol with a full medical summary.
Just make sure the medical notations on scene were in her sheet.
Her A&O x0/4 remains as per our initial assessment.
They know she has ongoing issues with the leg.
Will do. Thanks for everything.
She was handed off professionally.
She's ready for transport without further interventions or delays.
I'll wait outside and let you know how she does.
She was A&O x0/4 per our notes.
Her vital signs for the second time-blood pressure didn't falter much.
Her memory is noted in EMT records and consistent with expectations.
No procedure replication is needed based on the scene.
Any changes will be addressed at the hospital.
Her overall condition per our rechecks shows no change-she's considered stable.
She's stable in her pain and movement.
Baseline for her is consistent with EMT charts.
Safety first, she's in good hands and on her way.
I hope she gets better.
Confirmed safe for hospital arrival.
